phase_metadata.description:
Master the architecture for storing and manipulating groups of
objects using Lists, Sets, and Maps.

learning_objectives: Distinguish between List, Set, and Map interfaces
Select appropriate implementations like ArrayList, HashSet, and HashMap
Iterate through collections using Iterator and Enhanced For-loops

Utilize
the Collections utility class for sorting and searching

Implement Comparable
and Comparator for custom sorting logic

Understand the memory and performance
implications of different collection types
introduction:
In Java, an array has a fixed size which cannot be changed
after creation. In real-world applications, we often need data structures that
can grow and shrink dynamically. The Java Collections Framework provides a set
of high-performance interfaces and classes that implement commonly used data
structures like dynamic arrays, linked lists, hash tables, and trees, allowing
you to manage groups of objects with ease.

concept_explanation.what_is_it:
The Collections Framework is a unified architecture for
representing and manipulating collections. It contains interfaces (like List,
Set, Map), implementations (classes like ArrayList, HashSet), and algorithms
(methods like sort, binarySearch).
concept_explanation.why_do_we_need_it:
1. Dynamic Sizing:
Collections grow as needed. 2. Performance: High-performance implementations of
data structures. 3. Interoperability: Standardized way to pass groups of objects
between APIs.
concept_explanation.how_it_works:
Collections store only object references.
Primitives are automatically converted to their Wrapper classes (e.g., int to
Integer) via Autoboxing. The hierarchy starts with the 'Collection' interface,
which is extended by List and Set, while Map follows its own structure.
concept_explanation.core_interfaces:
1. List: Ordered collection, allows duplicates (ArrayList,
LinkedList). 2. Set: Unordered collection, forbids duplicates (HashSet,
TreeSet). 3. Map: Key-Value pairs, unique keys (HashMap, TreeMap).
concept_explanation.memory_behavior:
Collections live on the Heap. An ArrayList uses a dynamic
array that resizes by creating a new larger array and copying elements when
full. A HashMap uses an array of 'buckets' (nodes) where elements are stored
based on their hash code.

syntax_breakdown.generics_angle_brackets:
ensures
type safety, preventing non-specified objects from being added.
syntax_breakdown.list_add_method: Adds an element to the end of the collection.
syntax_breakdown.map_put_method:
Stores a value associated with a specific key; if the key
exists, the value is updated.
syntax_breakdown.iterator:
An object that allows sequential
traversal of a collection and safe removal of elements during iteration.
syntax_breakdown.comparable_interface:
Used to define the 'natural ordering' of an object
(implemented inside the class).
syntax_breakdown.comparator_interface:
Used to define
'external' or multiple sorting logic (implemented as a separate class or
lambda).

rules_and_key_points:
Collections only store objects, not
primitives.

ArrayList is better for random access (get/set), while LinkedList
is better for frequent insertions/deletions.

HashSet does not guarantee any
iteration order.
TreeSet and TreeMap store elements in a sorted order.
HashMap allows one null key and multiple null values.

The Collections class
(plural) contains static utility methods, not to be confused with the Collection
interface (singular).

Elements in a Set or Keys in a Map must correctly
implement equals() and hashCode().

List indexes start at 0, just like
arrays.

The 'final' keyword on a collection variable prevents reassignment
but allows modification of contents.

Iterator's remove() method is the only
safe way to remove elements while looping.

code_example_1.title: Working with Lists and Sets
code_example_1.description:
Demonstrating dynamic growth in
ArrayList and duplicate prevention in HashSet.

code_example_2.title: HashMap and Custom Sorting
code_example_2.description:
Using
a Map for key-value pairs and sorting a list using a Comparator.

code_example_1.expected_output:
List Content: [Java, Python, Java]
Second Element:
Python
Set Content (No Duplicates): [Java, C++, Python]
Contains 'Java'?
true
Product 101: Laptop
Sorted Products: [Cable: $10.0, Keyboard: $50.0,
Monitor: $200.0]
code_example_1.step_by_step_dry_run:
1. Main method starts. 
2.
ArrayList is created. 'Java', 'Python', 'Java' are added. Order is
preserved.
3. list.get(1) retrieves 'Python'.
4. HashSet is created using the
List. It automatically removes the duplicate 'Java'.
5. 'C++' is added to the
Set. Order might change as HashSet is unordered.
6. HashMap is created. Key 101
is mapped to 'Laptop'.
7. Product list is created and populated.
8. A
Comparator is passed to products.sort(), which compares prices.
9. The list is
reordered from lowest to highest price.
code_example_1.memory_visualization:
Heap:
ArrayList object contains an internal array [Ref1, Ref2, Ref3]. Each Ref points
to a String object on the Heap. 
HashMap: Contains an array of nodes. A key
'101' is hashed to find an index. The node at that index stores the key 101 and
the value 'Laptop'. 
Stack: Reference variables 'list', 'set', and 'inventory'
point to their respective objects on the Heap.
real_world_use_cases:
Shopping Cart: Using a List to store added items in the order they were
picked.

Unique User IDs: Using a Set to store active session IDs to ensure no
duplicates.

Dictionary/Cache: Using a HashMap to store user profiles indexed
by their numeric ID for O(1) lookup speed.

Leaderboards: Using a TreeMap to
keep player scores sorted automatically.
common_mistakes:
Trying to use
primitive types (int, double) in angle brackets. (Use Integer, Double
instead).

Removing elements using a for-each loop, which throws
ConcurrentModificationException.

Forgetting that HashSet and HashMap do not
maintain order.

Using a custom object as a Key in a Map without overriding
hashCode() and equals().

Confusing the capacity of a collection with its size
(size is the number of elements it currently holds).
best_practices: Program to interfaces (e.g., List list = new ArrayList<>()).

Set an initial
capacity if the size is known to avoid frequent internal resizing.

Use
'isEmpty()' instead of 'size() == 0' for better readability.

Prefer the
for-each loop or Stream API for readability unless you need to remove
elements.

Use Collections.unmodifiableList() to protect data from being
changed by external classes.

faq.question:
What is the difference
between ArrayList and LinkedList?
faq.answer:
ArrayList uses a dynamic array
and is faster for accessing elements by index. LinkedList uses nodes and is
faster for adding/removing elements at the beginning or middle of the list.

faq.question: How does a HashSet detect duplicates?
faq.answer:
It uses the
hashCode() and equals() methods. When an object is added, its hashCode
determines its bucket. If a bucket already has items, equals() is called to
check if the new item is identical to existing ones.

faq.question:
Can a
Map have duplicate values?
faq.answer:
Yes. Keys must be unique, but multiple
different keys can point to the same value.

code_example_1.summary:
Phase 10 explored
the Java Collections Framework, a powerful set of tools for managing data. We
covered the differences between Lists (ordered), Sets (unique), and Maps
(key-value). We also learned how to use Iterators for safe traversal and how to
sort objects using the Comparable and Comparator interfaces.
key_takeaways: Collections are dynamic alternatives to arrays.
ArrayList is the default choice for most List needs.

Use HashMap for fast
data retrieval by key.

Comparable is for natural sorting; Comparator is for
custom sorting.

The Collections utility class provides algorithms like sort
and shuffle.
Generics  ensure type safety at compile time.
code_example_1.preview_of_the_next_topic:
In Phase 11, we will learn about Generics in
depth, understanding how to create our own type-safe classes, methods, and
interfaces.

questions.question:
Which Collection interface should be used if you want to store a
sequence of elements and access them by their position?
questions.answer:
The 'List'
interface should be used. It maintains the insertion order and allows positional
access through index-based methods like get(index).

questions.question:
Explain the difference between
Comparable and Comparator.
questions.answer:
Comparable is implemented by the class
itself (via compareTo method) to provide a natural sort order. Comparator is a
separate class or lambda (via compare method) used to define multiple or custom
sorting criteria without modifying the original class.

questions.question:
Write code to initialize an
ArrayList of Integers, add three numbers, and remove the second number.
questions.answer:
List nums = new ArrayList<>(); nums.add(10); nums.add(20);
nums.add(30); nums.remove(1);

questions.question:
Write a method that takes a String and returns a Map containing the
frequency of each character in that string.

What happens if you try to add an element to a List while iterating over it
using a standard for-each loop?
questions.answer:
It will throw a
'ConcurrentModificationException'. The for-each loop uses an internal iterator,
and modifying the collection structure during iteration (except through the
iterator's own remove method) is forbidden.

questions.question:
If you create a class 'User' and use it as a key
in a HashMap without overriding hashCode(), what is the risk?
questions.answer:
The
risk is that you won't be able to retrieve the value later. Even if two User
objects have identical data, they will have different default hash codes (based
on memory address), so the HashMap will look in the wrong bucket and return
null.

questions.question:
How
would you sort a List of Strings in descending order (Z to A) using the
Collections utility class?

Which Map
implementation would you use if you needed to keep the keys sorted
automatically? Provide the class name and one requirement for the keys.
questions.answer:
You should use 'TreeMap'. The requirement is that the keys must
either implement the 'Comparable' interface or a 'Comparator' must be provided
to the TreeMap constructor so it knows how to sort them.

mcqs.question: Which of these is NOT an interface in the Collections Framework?
Which collection type allows duplicate elements?

What is the output? List l = new
ArrayList<>(); l.add(1); l.add(2); l.add(1, 3); System.out.print(l);

What happens when you add a duplicate element
to a HashSet?

options.C:
The add() method returns false and the set
remains unchanged
options.D: The element is added at the end

mcqs.question: Which method is used to retrieve a value from a HashMap?

Find the Bug: List list = new
ArrayList();

options.B: Generics do not support primitive types like 'int'

options.D:
Key and Value must be
different types
